coredata.dc:title: Novel yellow solid-state fluorescent-emitting carbon dots with high quantum yield for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hai-Jiao
Hou, Wan-Yi
Hao, Yong-Wei
Jiang, Wen-Shuai
Chen, Hong-Li
Zhang, Qi-Qing
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) occurs in their aggregated state ascribed to direct π–π interactions or excessive resonance energy transfer (RET). Thus, CDs have been severely restricted for applications requiring phosphors that emit in the solid state, such as the fabrication of white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). In this report, novel CDs with bright solid-state fluorescence (SSF) were synthesized by simple microwave-assisted synthesis method, using 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane (cyclen) and citric acid as precursors. Under 365 nm UV light, these CDs emit bright yellow SSF, indicating they successfully overcome the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) effect. When the excitation wavelength (λex) is fixed at 450 nm, the emission peak of the CDs is centered at 546 nm with the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage chromaticity (CIE) coordinates of (0.43, 0.55), which means that they can be combined with a blue-emitting chip in order to fabricate WLEDs. More importantly, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of these CDs powder reached 48% at λex of 450 nm, which was much higher than many previously reported SSF-emitting CDs and indicating their high light conversion ability in solid-state. Thanks to the excellent optical property of these CDs powder, they were successfully used in the preparation of high-performance WLEDs. This study not only enriches SSF-emitting CD-based nanomaterials with good prospects for application, but also provides valuable reference for subsequent research on the synthesis of solid-state fluorescent CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescence
White light-emitting diodes
Microwave-assisted synthesis method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of CyCDs and the intuitive picture of CyCDs under daylight and UV light.
(a) XPS of CyCDs. (b) FTIR of CyCDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CyCDs powder. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CyCDs in aqueous solution.
(a) and (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CyCDs powder. (c) and (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of CyCDs in aqueous solution.
(a) and (b) The fluorescence-decay curves of the CyCDs in aqueous solution (Ex = 450 nm, Em = 543 nm) and solid state (Ex = 450 nm, Em = 546 nm).

(a) Emission spectrum and the corresponding chromaticity coordinate of CyCDs fabricated LEDs. (b) Emission spectrum and the corresponding chromaticity coordinate of CyCDs/(Sr,Ca)AlSiN3:Eu composite fabricated LEDs. Insert: Intuitive picture of the related LEDs.

head.ce:title: Novel yellow solid-state fluorescent-emitting carbon dots with high quantum yield for white light-emitting diodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Material
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots powder with strong yellow solid-state fluorescence was prepared via a simple microwave-assisted synthesis method for successfully applied to fabricate WLEDs.

Fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) occurs in their aggregated state ascribed to direct π–π interactions or excessive resonance energy transfer (RET). Thus, CDs have been severely restricted for applications requiring phosphors that emit in the solid state, such as the fabrication of white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). In this report, novel CDs with bright solid-state fluorescence (SSF) were synthesized by simple microwave-assisted synthesis method, using 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane (cyclen) and citric acid as precursors. Under 365 nm UV light, these CDs emit bright yellow SSF, indicating they successfully overcome the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) effect. When the excitation wavelength (λex) is fixed at 450 nm, the emission peak of the CDs is centered at 546 nm with the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage chromaticity (CIE) coordinates of (0.43, 0.55), which means that they can be combined with a blue-emitting chip in order to fabricate WLEDs. More importantly, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of these CDs powder reached 48% at λex of 450 nm, which was much higher than many previously reported SSF-emitting CDs and indicating their high light conversion ability in solid-state. Thanks to the excellent optical property of these CDs powder, they were successfully used in the preparation of high-performance WLEDs. This study not only enriches SSF-emitting CD-based nanomaterials with good prospects for application, but also provides valuable reference for subsequent research on the synthesis of solid-state fluorescent CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) attracted a lot of interest in recent years due to factors including low power consumption, their high luminous efficiency, long functional lifetimes and remarkable energy efficiency [1,2]. These superior features make WLEDs the fourth-generation lighting source in the 21st century [3]. At present, the common phosphors for the manufacture of WLEDs are semiconductor quantum dots and rare-earth-based phosphors. For example, high-performance WLEDs can be manufactured by combining yellow-emitting rare-earth-based phosphors with blue chips blue-emitting GaN chips [4,5]. Although these phosphors have high light conversion ability, some problems remain to be solved, such as the fact that rare earth element based phosphors are nonrenewable and expensive, and that semiconductor quantum dots also contain some potentially toxic heavy metals. Therefore, the development of low-cost, environmentally friendly, and high-performance phosphors is an important goal of the current WLEDs industry. Recently, much research has focused carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials. Scientists hope that the properties of CDs, such as their efficient light conversion, excellent photo-stability, low cost, and low toxicity, can enable CDs to be substituted for semiconductor quantum dots and rare earth element based phosphors [6–9]. Although many type of CDs have been synthesized and used in sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine, solar cells and catalysis, unfortunately, like many organic dyes, fluorescence quenching occurs in their aggregated state ascribed to direct π–π interactions or excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) [7,10]. The aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching (ACQ) phenomenon suggests that although most synthesized CDs have good fluorescence properties in aqueous solution, but they may occur fluorescence quenching in solid state. This disadvantage seriously restricts CDs in the applications of fabricating LEDs, because LEDs generally require phosphors to emit in the solid state. Therefore, the synthesis of solid-state fluorescence (SSF)-emitting CDs has become a key goal.

In order to obtain SSF-emitting CDs, several solutions have proposed. One popular solution is to use some solid-state matrices as a dispersion medium like water to uniformly disperse CDs, so that the CDs do not aggregate with each other, thus avoiding the quenching of fluorescence [11]. For example, Liu et al achieved efficient SSF-emitting CDs by physically embedding the CDs into a silica matrix [12]. They hypothesized that the CD particles in the composites maintain adequate distance so that ACQ effect is prevented. Recently, Yang et al immobilized CDs in a polymer (PVP) to create a full-color CD/PVP powder with strong solid-state fluorescence [13]. The authors suggested that the polymer matrix of PVP uniformly disperses the CDs particles and thus avoids the ACQ effect. Other solid-state scaffolds, such as starch, sodium polyacrylate, SBA-15, and SiO2, are also used to suppress the ACQ effect [14–17]. Although the use of solid scaffolds to overcome the ACQ effect has achieved good results, these methods suffer from problems with low loading fraction of CDs and difficulty retaining the outstanding fluorescent properties of CDs solution [18]. Therefore, the direct synthesis of CDs with solid state fluorescence is a significant open question.

In order to directly construct CDs with solid-state fluorescence, a popular strategy has been proposed: Using amphiphilic molecules or polymers during synthesis leads to some larger residues being retained on the surface of CDs [19,20]. This avoids close contact between CDs particles and therefore leads to SSF-emitting CDs [20–22]. Although some types of CDs with SSF properties were successfully prepared using the above strategy, SSF from CDs also faces some serious challenges, such as relatively low quantum yield (QY) in the solid state compared to other fluorophores [19]. The low quantum yield of SSF-emitting CDs seriously impedes their practical application. Therefore, scientists still urgently need to develop more types of SSF-emitting CDs with high QY are still needed to meet the requirements of various applications. In this paper, we propose a simple microwave-assisted synthesis method for the synthesis of strong yellow SSF-emitting CDs by using 1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane (cyclen) and citric acid as starting materials. To our delight, under an excitation wavelength of 450 nm, its maximum emission wavelength is centered at 546 nm, with the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage chromaticity (CIE) coordinates of (0.43, 0.55), meaning that it emits yellow SSF. Importantly, the absolute QY of these CDs powder reached 48% at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm, which was much higher than many previously reported SSF-emitting CDs and indicating their high light conversion ability in solid-state [19]. Thanks to their excellent optical properties, they have been successfully applied in the preparation of WLEDs by combining with a commercially available 450 nm blue-emitting GaN chip.

1,4,7,10-tetraazacyclododecane (cyclen, 98%+) were purchased from adamas-beta. Citric acid were obtained from innochem. All chemicals were obtained from commercial suppliers and were used without further purification. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on JEOL JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscope. FTIR spectra was conducted on a FT-IR Nicolet 380 spectrometer. The Thermo ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer was utilized to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). Fluorescence spectra of the CDs in aqueous solution were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan). Fluorescence spectra of the CDs powder were recorded on a Spectrofluorometer FS5 at 298 K. LEDs was measured on a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absolute quantum yield of the CDs were measured by Absolute PL Quantum Yield Masurement System C9920-02G (Hamamatsu Photonics).

Briefly, 1 g cyclen and 2 g citric acid were dissolved in 50 ml water, and then transferred to a 250 ml beaker. Then place the beaker in the center of a domestic microwave rotary plate and heat for 10 min (800 W). After microwave irradiation, 50 ml water was added and ultrasound for an appropriate time. Then microwave irradiation for another 10 min and the solid-state crude products can be obtained. Then CDs powder was obtained by grinding the solid-state products gently in a mortar.

Firstly, the CyCDs were mixed uniformly with silicone and put in a vacuum chamber to remove the bubbles. Then the mixture was coated on the commercially available 450 nm blue-emitting GaN chip. Finally the WLEDs was obtained after 100 °C drying.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the CDs were synthesized from the precursors comprising of cyclen and citric acid via a simple one-step MW-assisted method. During the process of microwave irradiation, the precursors react with each other through condensation reaction, and then dehydrated and carbonized to form the CDs. After microwave irradiation, solid crude products can be formed. Then the CDs powder with SSF properties can be obtained by grinding the crude products gently, which we term CyCDs (Cyclen based CDs). Under UV-light, the CyCDs emitted yellow SSF, indicating these CyCDs overcome the ACQ effect successfully. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired to observe the morphology of CyCDs. As exhibited in Fig. S1, CyCDs show quasi-spherical shapes with a particle size of 1–4 nm. The lattice fringes of CyCDs can be seen with high resolution microscopy images. The lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm, which is in close agreement with that of the (100) planes within the graphite. Meanwhile, the XRD pattern of CyCDs displays a very broad peak at approximately 2θ = 16° with a shoulder peak at ~21° (Fig. S2), which indicates that the CyCDs are composed of an amorphous surface and a crystalline carbon core [23,24].

The XPS spectra were performed to understand the possible structure and chemical composition of CyCDs (Fig. 2 a). The XPS full spectrum displays three typical characteristic peaks at 284.5 eV, 399.3 eV and 531.4 eV, which belong to C1s, N1s and O1s peaks. The C1s spectrum of CyCDs exhibits three carbon states of CC/CC at 284.1 eV, CO/CN at 285.1 eV and CO at 287.0 eV, respectively [25–27]. N1s spectrum exhibits two main peaks at 398.9 eV and 400.5 eV, suggesting to the presence of pyridinic-like and pyrrolic-like N [28,29]. The result confirms that polyaromatic structures containing CN were formed in CyCDs. The O1s bands can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.2 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/CO, respectively [30–32]. XPS analysis also provided the percentage of atoms in CyCDs (Table S1). Compared with the percentage of atoms in the starting materials, the carbon and nitrogen content in CyCDs increased, while the oxygen content decreased. The results may indicate that dehydration and recombination of raw materials occurred during the microwave irradiation process. FTIR spectra were used to further investigate the surface groups of CyCDs. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the spectrum displays strong and broad absorption peaks at about 3430 cm−1, which are attributed to NH and OH stretching vibrations [33]. The peaks at ~1722 cm−1 and ~1630 cm−1 confirm the presence of the CO and OCN bond, which indicates that citric acid and cyclen are successfully covalently conjugated through amide bonds.

To determine the optical properties of CyCDs, UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, obviously, CyCDs powder show two broad absorption peaks in the range of 300–400 nm and 400–500 nm. The former absorption peak in the ultraviolet region may be assigned to the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain in carbon core [34,35], while the latter in the visible region most likely to be attributed to n → π* transitions of CN/CO and/or CN [28,36]. The excitation spectra of CyCDs powder also investigated (Fig. S3). CyCDs powder shows a broad excitation peak between ~400 nm and ~500 nm, which matches their UV–Vis absorption spectrum. These results indicate that the broad absorption peaks in the visible region may be responsible for the fluorescence emission of CyCDs powder. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CyCDs in aqueous solution was also measured (Fig. 3b). Similar to the absorption spectrum of CyCDs powder, these CDs also exhibit a broad absorption peak in the range of 400–500 nm.

We then studied the photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra of CyCDs powder (Fig. 4 a-b and Fig. S4a). When excited at 365 nm UV light, two fluorescence emission peaks were observed. One emission peak is centered at 422 nm and the other is centered at 544 nm. In addition, the intensity of the emission peak at the low-energy region is much stronger than that in the high-energy region. From the PL spectrum, the CIE coordinates of CyCDs powder was calculated as (0.39, 0.53), indicating that they emitted yellow fluorescence (Fig. S5). Previous reports have shown that the emission peaks of CDs may vary with the excitation wavelength (λex) [37,38]. Therefore, the fluorescence emission spectra of CyCDs powder at different λex were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4b, the emission intensity of CyCDs powder first increases and then decreases with the increase of λex. In addition, as the λex increases, the weak emission peak located at 422 nm finally disappears, while the peak located at ~544 nm gradually increases and reaches its maximum at λex of 485 nm. Furthermore, a red-shift is observed in the normalized PL spectra when λex increases, which suggests that the CDs powder also have tunable PL emission characteristics, similar to those previous reports in aqueous solution [7]. In addition, the emission spectra of CyCDs in aqueous solution were also evaluated (Fig. 4c-d and Fig. S4b). Similar to the PL spectra from CyCDs powder, CyCDs also show two emission peak in aqueous solution. With the red shift of λex, the emission intensity of CyCDs in aqueous solution gradually increases. When the excitation wavelength is 445 nm, the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum, and the maximum emission wavelength is 543 nm. Moreover, considering the mass ratio of cyclen and citric acid may affect their PL properties, the CDs at other two mass ratio (w/w, cyclen:citric acid, named as CyCDs1 and CyCDs2) of 1:1 and 1:3 were also prepared. As shown in the Fig. S6, CyCDs1 powder shows white fluorescence, CyCDs2 powder displays yellow-green fluorescence, and compared with CyCDs powder, the fluorescence intensity of CyCDs1 and CyCDs2 powder is relatively weak. Meanwhile, their spectra in solid state and aqueous solution also were evaluated, and results suggest that the PL property of these CDs are different, which indicates that their optical properties will be seriously affected by the mass ratio (Fig. S7–8).

Because of the ACQ effect, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in an aggregated state is generally low. In particular, SSF-emitting CDs materials prepared through solid matrix dispersion often lose part of their fluorescence, resulting in a lower quantum yield (QY) than that obtained aqueous solution [18]. Based on the PL spectra of CyCDs powder and considering that commercially 450 nm blue-emitting GaN chip will select for subsequent production of white LEDs, the absolute QY of CyCDs in aqueous solution and solid state were calculated under 450 nm. The absolute QY of the CyCDs powder was measured to be 48% (Ex = 450 nm), suggesting high fluorescence emission efficiency of CyCDs powder under excitation of 450 nm. The QY of CyCDs is not only higher than many previously reported SSF-emitting CDs, but even higher than many CDs in aqueous solution [39–42]. The absolute QY of CyCDs in aqueous solution was measured to be 20.7% (Ex = 450 nm), which is lower than that of CyCDs powder. These results show that CyCDs not only successfully conquered the ACQ effect, but also achieved ultra-high solid-state fluorescence emission efficiency. In addition, the absolute QY of CyCDs1 and CyCDs2 powder were also measured and results show that the absolute QY of CyCDs1 and CyCDs2 powder are 13.8% and 32.1%, respectively, lower than that of CyCDs powder. The high QY of these CDs in solid-state will be much beneficial to solid-state lighting applications.

At present, the PL mechanism of CDs is still not clear. According to previous reports, the PL mechanism of CyCDs can possibly be attributed to the surface states and carbon core [43,44]. Meanwhile, the carbon core states usually emit at shorter wavelengths [45]. Since the CyCDs have two emission peaks in aqueous solution, we infer that these CDs may have two types of emissive sites (such as surface states and graphitizing carbon cores). The time-resolved PL spectrum of CyCDs in aqueous solution were evaluated (Fig. 5 a) and results that the fluorescence attenuation curves are well fitted by a double-exponential decay with PL lifetimes of 2.96 ns and 11.67 ns, indicating that there are two luminescent components in CyCDs, which is consistent with its PL spectra in aqueous solution. Then the time-resolved PL spectrum of CyCDs powder also was evaluated (Fig. 5b) and the results indicated the fluorescence attenuation curves are well fitted by a single-exponential decay with PL lifetimes of 7.70 ns, indicating that only one radiative channels opening for the emission in solid [46]. Based on the above results and considering the large overlap between the absorption, excitation spectrum and PL spectrum of CyCDs in aqueous solution (Fig. S9), we believe that when CyCDs are in a state of aggregation, energy resonance transfer, self-absorption and recombination over the coupled surface states may occur. This may have led to strong enhancement of yellow fluorescence emission of CyCDs in solid-state and weaken their blue fluorescence emission, ultimately resulted in strong yellow fluorescence emission with high QY [18,21,47–49]. Further research is needed to achieve an in-depth understanding of the mechanisms.

The development of WLEDs is a very important topic due to their great potential in lighting and display [13]. An efficient and commercial route for manufacturing WLEDs is to combine commercially blue-emitting GaN chip with yellow fluorescence-emitting phosphors. The CyCDs powder exhibits high QY of 48% with the emission maximum around 546 nm and CIE coordinates of (0.43, 0.55) under λex of 450 nm, indicating that the CyCDs powder emitted strong yellow fluorescence and can fabricate WLEDs with a commercially 450 nm blue-emitting GaN chip (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 6 a, there are two emission peaks in the spectrum of prepared WLEDs. The emission peaks located at 450 nm are from the GaN chip, while those at 546 nm are from CyCDs. These results suggest that CyCDs can efficiently convert the blue-light from the GaN chip into yellow light, producing white light through the combination of the two emissions. As depicted in Fig. 6a, bright white illumination is successfully achieved by the prepared device. Based on CIE 1931 chromaicity coordinates, the CIE coordinates of WLEDs was calculated as (0.30, 0.34). This is close to the pure white light CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.33) and suggests white light emission. In addition, the luminescence efficiency of WLEDs is 25.5 lm/w, which is comparable to previously reported values [1,50–53]. Further, in order to improve the color rendering index (CRI), CyCDs were mixed with a small amount of commercial red fluorescence-emitted (Sr,Ca)AlSiN3:Eu powder to fabricate WLEDs. As expected, the color rendering index of LEDs reached 82.1 and CIE coordinates of the CyCDs reaches (0.33, 0.32) after adding (Sr,Ca)AlSiN3:Eu powder (Fig. 6b), suggesting the LEDs achieve high performance white lighting. Other related parameters of the LEDs also were evaluated (Table S2) and these results demonstrate CyCDs may use as a promising phosphors for fabricating WLEDs.

In summary, a simple microwave-assisted synthesis method was proposed for synthesis of SSF-emitting CDs by using cyclen and citric acid as starting materials. The as-prepared CyCDs powder can emit bright yellow fluorescence. Under the λex of 450 nm, the absolute QY of CyCDs powder is measured to be 48%, suggesting high fluorescence emission efficiency of CyCDs powder. The QY of CyCDs is not only higher than many previously reported SSF-emitting CDs, but even higher than many CDs in aqueous solution. The absolute QY of CyCDs in aqueous solution was measured to be only 20.7% (Ex = 450 nm), which is much lower than that of CyCDs powder. These results indicate that as-prepared CyCDs not only successfully conquered the ACQ effect, but also achieved ultra-high solid-state fluorescence emission efficiency. Because of the excellent optical properties of CyCDs, they were successfully employed to manufacture high-performance WLEDs via combining with a commercially available 450 nm blue-emitting GaN chip. The research not only provides a novel SSF-emitting CDs, but also confirmed that the CyCDs developed herein have good prospects for application.

Hai-Jiao Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Wan-Yi Hou: Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yong-Wei Hao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wen-Shuai Jiang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hong-Li Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Qi-Qing Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank financial support from the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Xinxiang Medical University (XYBSKYZZ201716), the Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (20A150034) and the Key Technologies R&D Program of Henan Province (NO. 202102310347, NO.172102310034 and NO. 182102311130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119340.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct white emissive Cl-doped graphene quantum dots-based flexible film as a single luminophore for remote tunable UV-WLEDs
Chem. Eng. J.

Han, Direct white emissive Cl-doped graphene quantum dots-based flexible film as a single luminophore for remote tunable UV-WLEDs, Chem. Eng. J. 361 (2019) 773-782. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.12.131.

Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

Dyes Pigments

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and luminescence properties of novel red-emitting Na2ZnSiO4:Eu3+ phosphor with intense 5D0 → 7F4 transition and high quantum yield
J. Alloys Compd.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile approach to solid-state white emissive carbon dots and their application in UV-excitable and single-component-based white LEDs
Nanomaterials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting
Nano Energy

J. Mater. Chem. C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-quenching-resistant carbon dots powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and their applications in light-emitting diodes and fingerprints detection
